html:
Honest Roof Documentation After a Permian Basin Hailstorm

Spring hail moving across Ector County and the surrounding oil-patch towns can hit a building hard enough to trigger a claim, or leave marks that look alarming from the ground but never break the roof's ability to shed water. A property owner or restoration company calling us after a storm needs a straight answer about which one happened, not a sales pitch tuned to what an insurance payout might cover.

Cosmetic Hail Damage Versus Functional Damage

On a single-ply membrane, hail impact can leave surface bruising without breaking the membrane's weatherability. On a metal panel roof, it can leave dimpling that looks worse than it is. On a modified bitumen or built-up roof, granule loss and cracked coating are common cosmetic effects of an impact that did not penetrate the membrane. None of that automatically means the roof needs replacement, and none of it automatically means the roof is fine either. We test and document specific conditions, membrane thickness readings, granule embedment, seam integrity near impact points, rather than making a call from a ground-level look or a drone photo alone.

The distinction matters because an owner who replaces a roof that only needed cosmetic repair overspends, and an owner who walks away from functional hail damage because it looked minor ends up with a leak claim a year later that is harder to tie back to the original storm.

The Storm-Chaser Problem

West Texas hail season brings out-of-state crews who work a neighborhood for a few weeks after a major storm, sign owners to contracts before an adjuster ever sees the roof, and are gone before a warranty issue surfaces. We are not that. We are a local contractor with a presence in this market before the storm and after the claim closes, which matters when a roof needs a warranty call two years later or a follow-up inspection ahead of the next hail season.

Documentation Timing and the Adjuster Visit

What we document, and when, affects how a claim gets evaluated. If a roof needs emergency tarp or dry-in to stop active water intrusion, that happens first, with photos of the damage before and after temporary protection goes on. If the roof is stable and the damage is not actively leaking, we document conditions in detail before any adjuster visit so the owner has an independent record that does not depend on timing or weather matching up with the carrier's inspection date.

We do not promise claim approval or a specific payout, and we are direct with owners about that up front. What we provide is a factual, measured record of roof condition, damage location, and probable cause that an owner, a public adjuster, or a restoration company can use in the claims process.

What We Document on Every Storm Call

  • Impact location, spacing, and density mapped against roof slope and prevailing wind direction
  • Membrane or panel condition at each impact point, beyond a general damage estimate
  • Pre-existing conditions and prior repairs, so old damage is not attributed to the current storm
  • Drainage and flashing condition, since wind-driven hail often damages edge metal before it damages the field
  • Photographs with measurement references and roof plan location for every documented area
  • A written scope separating emergency stabilization from longer-term repair or replacement recommendations

Working Alongside Restoration Companies and Adjusters

Restoration companies coordinating a larger property loss, water intrusion into occupied space below the roof, mechanical damage, interior finishes, often bring us in specifically for the roof scope because that work needs a roofing contractor's eye, not a general contractor's assumption about membrane condition. We provide our findings in a format that plugs into a restoration company's broader project file and are available for adjuster walk-throughs where an owner wants a contractor present to explain what was found.

Emergency Response After Wind and Hail Events

When a storm has actively opened a roof, temporary protection comes before anything else. We prioritize emergency tarp and dry-in calls ahead of routine inspections, because stopping ongoing water intrusion protects both the building interior and the eventual claim, an active, undocumented leak that continues for days is harder to scope accurately than one that got dried in and photographed the same day it happened.

Questions Owners Ask After a Storm

Will you tell us if the damage doesn't justify a claim?

Yes. If impact marks are cosmetic and have not compromised the roof's function, we say so directly. Filing a claim for damage that will not support a payout wastes the owner's time and can affect future premiums for no benefit.

How soon after a storm should the roof get inspected?

As soon as it is safe to access the roof, ideally within a few days while impact marks are fresh and before additional weather or foot traffic changes the evidence. Active leaks get prioritized immediately regardless of storm timing.

Do you work directly with our restoration company or public adjuster?

Yes. We provide documentation, scope, and are available for joint site visits when an owner or restoration company wants a roofing contractor present during the claims process.

Can you repair the roof before the claim is settled?

Emergency stabilization to stop active water intrusion happens regardless of claim status. Full repair or replacement typically waits for claim resolution unless the owner chooses to proceed independently of the insurance timeline.

What keeps this from turning into an inflated claim?

We scope to documented, verifiable conditions and do not pad damage assessments to increase a payout. Our documentation reflects what the roof actually needs, which protects the owner's credibility with the carrier over the life of the policy.
